Jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they provide skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. This strongly supports weighting the intrinsic structure of the work, not just pay or title.

**“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007).**

**“Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It” — O’Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018).**

- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham, 1976.** This paper shows that autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback predict internal motivation and job satisfaction. It supports prioritizing interesting, self-directed work, but not treating “interesting” as the only variable.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Ryan & Deci, 2000.** The paper argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are core psychological needs behind motivation and well-being. Both OpenAI-style research depth and Meta-style team leadership could satisfy these needs depending on the actual role design.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.** This meta-analysis finds that controlling external rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. It cautions against moving primarily for money if the new role reduces genuine intellectual engagement.

- **“Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do” — Amabile, 1997.** The paper argues that intrinsic motivation is central to creativity, particularly in complex knowledge work. For research engineering, it implies that losing problem-interest may reduce both satisfaction and creative output.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010.** This meta-analysis finds only a modest relationship between pay level and job satisfaction. It suggests that a somewhat higher salary should usually be weighed less than day-to-day work quality, autonomy, and fit.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023.** This paper reconciles earlier findings by showing that well-being generally keeps rising with income, but with heterogeneous effects across people. It argues against ignoring salary entirely, while still implying that salary gains may be smaller than differences in daily work experience.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.** This meta-analysis finds that fit strongly predicts job satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. The relevant question is whether you fit better with frontier research execution or with leading a team in a less intellectually exciting domain.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997.** The paper distinguishes people who view work as a job, career, or calling, with calling-oriented workers reporting higher satisfaction. It suggests that if your OpenAI work feels like a calling, replacing it with a merely better career move may be costly.

- **“The Value of Working Conditions in the United States and Implications for the Structure of Wages” — Maestas, Mullen, Powell, von Wachter & Wenger, 2018.** This paper estimates that workers place large monetary value on non-wage job attributes such as autonomy, meaning, schedule control, and work environment. It supports treating “interesting work” and “leadership opportunity” as real compensation, not soft extras.

- **“Valuing Alternative Work Arrangements” — Mas & Pallais, 2017.** This paper shows that workers are often willing to trade pay for preferred work arrangements, though preferences vary substantially. It implies there is no universal rule to focus almost exclusively on interestingness; your own preference intensity matters.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton, 2015.** This paper finds that bosses have large effects on worker productivity. It suggests that leading a small team can be high-leverage and meaningful, even if the object-level work is less interesting.

- **“Leadership Is Associated with Lower Levels of Stress” — Sherman et al., 2012.** This paper finds that leaders can have lower stress markers than non-leaders, partly because leadership increases perceived control. It supports the idea that a management role may improve autonomy and agency, not merely add burden.

- **“Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction” — Schkade & Kahneman, 1998.** This paper shows that people overestimate the impact of salient life differences when forecasting happiness. Applied here, you may overweight either “Meta pays more and lets me lead” or “OpenAI work is more interesting” unless you compare ordinary weeks in each role.

- **“Affective Forecasting” — Wilson & Gilbert, 2003.** This review shows that people often mispredict how future events will affect their happiness and how quickly they will adapt. It suggests making the decision from concrete daily activities, growth paths, coworkers, autonomy, and likely regrets rather than abstract prestige or role labels.
